summary: A field edge's `to` widens to a declared non-empty set of target kinds: a one-element set resolves bare names exactly as today, a multi-element set demands the kind-qualified address always

edit.description: The authoring type. `EdgeField.to` (46, doc 45 'The target kind's name — the far end of the edge') widens from `string` to the set — and the model's non-emptiness is TS-expressible (`readonly [string, ...string[]]`), which is where it costs nothing: the keystroke is the SDK's declared enforcement placement (distribution.md), so an empty set failing only at engine load would be the weaker spelling of a bar the type can hold. 0029 forecloses the friendlier `string | readonly string[]` union — it rules the type widens to the set, and a permanent two-spelling surface is the no-middles violation. `edgeFields` rides `KindFacts` (152) and `EmbeddedMemberValue` (346) as `readonly EdgeField[]` and needs no edit at either site.

The lowering. `assemblyFactRows` (348-355) copies `to: edge.to` verbatim into the `edge` assembly fact (352) — one line, and the whole seam: the shape it writes is the shape `src/drift.rs`'s `AssemblyFactRow` reads, so the two spell one column type or the lock is malformed.

Regenerated from `src/drift.rs`'s `AssemblyFactRow` by ts-rs, never hand-edited — it moves because the row's `to` column changes type. `tests/seam_bindings_current.rs` is the gate that catches a stale binding.

The lock wire, and the legacy read it owes. `AssemblyFactRow.to` (2828, doc 2826 'An `edge` fact's target kind') becomes the array column; `to_table` (3574-3576) writes it and `from_table` (3586, via `opt_str`) reads it. **The legacy read is owed, not optional** — pipeline.md, 'The lock': an upgraded engine owes a committed lock a robust read and a canonical rewrite. A bare `to = "decision"` is the lossless spelling of the one-element set (0029's degenerate case) and reads as `["decision"]`; the file is never patched, and the next emit rewrites it whole in canonical form. `templates_from_table`'s legacy normalize (08484b3) is the shipped shape to follow rather than reinvent — including its narrowness: a `to` that is neither string nor array-of-strings still refuses loud as a `RowError`, so the gate stays tight against a genuinely corrupt lock. `opt_str_array` (3029) is the existing array reader to compose with, not to duplicate. Field evidence the bare spelling really shipped: `examples/base-harness/.temper/lock.toml:317-326` carries two.

`Edge.to` (52) widens to the set. Its doc (49-51) — 'The artifact kind the reference resolves to — the edge *target*. The target kind must be one `temper` models' — is singular twice over and goes false with the field; it moves with it. **Carry the accepted-debt rider this entry is now the carrier for** (open-questions, 4144b20's retirement): the test doc comment at **234** ('Unlike `effective`, a custom kind has no embedded default to override…') cites `compose::effective`, a symbol 4144b20 retired — verified absent from this file, and the surviving `effective contract` hits elsewhere are the ordinary English word, not the symbol. Re-word it off the retired name; the rider discharges only when an entry names it, never when the file is merely opened.

The lift, and the empty-set call. `edges_from_declarations` (1907-1922) maps each `edge` assembly fact into a `compose::Edge`; `edge_column(fact.to.clone(), "to")?` (1918) is the required-column refusal an absent column already takes (`edge_column` itself at 1926). An EMPTY set is the model's own violation (contract.md, 'edge': a **non-empty** set) and refuses at load in that same class — an edge declaring no target kind can never resolve, and loading it would be a silent degrade against intent.md invariant 6. State the call in the commit body.

The normalizer 0029 names, and every reader of the widened field. All cites re-derived at 01337ff, which moved this file +142. `target_identity` (1244, doc 1234-1243) is the one home: today it strips a `to_kind:` prefix and returns any other spelling whole so it dangles under its authored name. Over a set it must answer WHICH declared kind resolved, not the identity alone — the one-element case (bare name or `kind:name`) behaving exactly as EMBEDDED-EDGE-DEGREE-SEAM (c607fad) built it, the multi-element case resolving only a `kind:name` whose kind is in the declared set, and a BARE name in a multi-element set resolving to nothing (0029: resolution depends on the written text, never the member population — so no uniqueness inference, even when one member would happen to be unique). Its two callers move with it: `check` (117-145) and `resolved_edges` (936-967) each build one `targets` set from `by_kind.get(edge.to)` (127, 946), which over a set becomes a per-kind lookup — `ResolvedEdge.to` (961) is a `(kind, id)` pair and must name the kind that actually resolved, so the tuple shape itself needs no change. Verified at 01337ff: the new `mention-reachable` judge reads `ResolvedEdge.to` (379-405), never `compose::Edge.to`, so it is untouched by this widening and adds no reader. `admissibility` clause (b) (176-187, doc 152-155) and `is_admissible` (1219) both test `by_kind.contains_key(edge.to)`; over a set the bar is EVERY declared element modeled — an unmodeled element is a route that can never resolve, the fault is the declaration's, and the finding names the element, not the set. The three diagnostics interpolating `edge.to` into prose (171, 183, and 1265 via `dangling`) render a set now.

Fixture migration, mechanical: the `waypoint` kind's six single-kind edge fields (`edgeFields` at 72-79 — `{ field: "to_skill", to: "skill" }` at 73 and five siblings through 78) each re-spell as the one-element set. Spelling only — the fixture's coverage (an embedded format's rendered target facts) is unchanged and no assertion moves.

Two fixture sites, same mechanical migration: `edgeFields: [{ field: "source", to: "rule" }]` at **1089 and 1177** re-spell as the one-element set. Re-verified on disk at 01337ff, which shifted both +2 from the 1087/1175 they were last scoped at. Note the sibling at 1203 is an expected `{ fact: "edge", … to: "rule" }` assembly-fact ROW, not an authored `edgeFields` entry — it moves with the lowered column's shape, not with the authoring type.
Third fixture site: `edgeFields: [{ field: "source", to: "rule" }]` (220), the same one-element re-spelling.

tests.asserts: the addressing rule both ways: a multi-element `to` resolves a `kind:name` address to that kind and DANGLES a bare name even when it would be unique across the set; a one-element `to` resolves a bare name exactly as today; an admissibility finding names the unmodeled element of a set, not the set

the widened `to` column round-trips as an array, a committed lock's legacy bare-string `to` reads as the one-element set without the file being patched, and a `to` that is neither string nor string-array still refuses loud

acceptance: A field edge declares its target set: a one-element `to` behaves exactly as today (a bare name resolves within its one kind), a multi-element `to` resolves only kind-qualified addresses and never infers from the member population, and a committed lock's legacy bare-string `to` reads as the singleton unpatched (contract.md, "edge": the kind declares the field's target as a non-empty set of kinds, the authored address naming which member of the declared set). `cargo test` green; `pnpm --dir sdk test` green.
notes: Derived from 0029 (f6fe385). Built-in lock untouched, re-verified at 01337ff: `fact = "edge"` count is 0 in src/builtin_lock.toml — no shipped kind declares an edge field — so the frozen lock does not re-derive. Legacy-read evidence: examples/base-harness/.temper/lock.toml:317-326 carries two bare-string `to` rows; that example's own kinds.ts (135, 182) needs the same re-spelling but is human territory outside build's fence — a `docs(example):` commit. scoped 3e02d96; cites re-derived 01337ff.

summary: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` cites a memory doc that now reads four hops: the gate constant and its own source disagree, and the doc sentence cannot settle which counting is temper's

gate.reason: A human probe rules the hop semantics — the doc text cannot. Fetched live at 420da04 (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-16): 'Imported files can recursively import other files, with a maximum depth of four hops.' Both readings survive that sentence: the root's own import is hop 1 and the chain caps at four edges (⇒ 4), or 'imported files ... recursively import' names a recursion four deep ATOP that first import (⇒ 5, today's constant). Only measuring the runtime's actual load depth decides it, the headless-testbed act that produced the `(mention-gate-containment)` evidence — never plan's to guess and never build's to fetch, because guessing here edits a live gate's semantics: a cap too low forges a dead-member finding on a member that loads, a cap too high suppresses a real one (intent.md invariant 2).

edit.description: The constant and the cite that justifies it. All cites re-derived at 01337ff, which moved this file +142. `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS = 5` (65) sits under a doc (61-64) asserting 'the `at-import` grammar is recursion-capped at five hops (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory, retrieved 2026-07-02)' — a number the live page does not say. temper's own indexing IS decidable on disk and needs no ruling: `live_members` (628-669) seeds every own-live member and runs `for _ in 0..MAX_IMPORT_HOPS` BFS rounds (655), one import edge per round, so the constant counts EDGES traversed from a live seed. What no reading of the disk settles is which edges the doc's 'four hops' counts — hence the park. Whatever the ruling: the cite is re-retrieved, re-dated, and re-worded to state the hop-counting it encodes, never left asserting a bare number its source does not carry. The two narration cites deferring to it — 526-527 ('hop-capped at [`MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`] as the format documents') and 625-626 ('capped at [`MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`] hops (the `at-import` recursion depth the format documents)') — carry the same claim and move with it, as does the propagation comment at 650-653 ('capped at the format's import recursion depth').

`the_import_closure_is_hop_capped` (1402, re-derived at 01337ff from the 1238 it was last scoped at) already pins the boundary mechanically rather than in a comment — `r1..r5` rescued from a live seed, `r6` dead — so it encodes today's 5 in the one place a flip cannot silently miss, and it is the test that moves if the ruling is four. Its comment (1405-1407) spells 'the format's five-hop import recursion' and carries the same claim as the constant's cite; re-word it to the ruled reading rather than leaving the fixture narrating the retired number.

tests.asserts: the import closure rescues a chain exactly as deep as the ruled cap and no deeper — the boundary hop pinned at the ruled number, in the ruled counting

acceptance: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS`, its doc cite, and the two narration cites that defer to it all agree with the live docs, and each states which hop-counting it encodes (spec-system.md, 'Form rules': an external fact carries its source and retrieval date where it is enforced, verified against current docs, never encoded from memory). The boundary hop stays pinned by a test at the ruled number. `cargo test` green.
notes: Park re-tested at 01337ff and holds — nothing ruled the hop semantics. 83fa520 moved graph.rs (+142) and tests/graph.rs (+166) without touching the constant or its cites, so every line cite above is re-derived while the claim is unchanged: `MAX_IMPORT_HOPS` reads 5 at graph.rs:65 under a cite claiming five. Stays parked: the constant may still be wrong, the one thing keeping it out of the staleness-rider class. rescoped 4a33911; re-cited 01337ff.

summary: The packaging remainder after the linux-x64+win32-x64 first cut: darwin binaries (Apple notarizing), the plugin/bundle + marketplace.json, and the binary↔npm version lockstep at the v0.1 tag

gate.reason: Human release actions: John's Apple notarizing for the darwin standalone binary + darwin optionalDependency package; the v0.1 launch tag that flips the binary's self-reported crate version (0.1.0) into lockstep with npm's 0.0.x. Channel 3 (plugin/bundle + marketplace.json) is a git repo + marketplace.json + free community web form (verified 2026-07-06, code.claude.com/docs) — no signing creds — but rides the same release-workflow build-out. release.yml (channel 2, linux-x64+win32-x64) + the SDK launcher/optionalDependencies + standalone tag assets shipped 07-11; the standalone-asset step is wired but unexercised until a tag fires.

edit.description: Extend the existing release.yml (linux-x64 + win32-x64 first cut, the darwin deferral stated at lines 7-9) with the darwin matrix targets (aarch64/x86_64-apple-darwin) + an Apple notarization step, and a channel-3 step producing/publishing the `temper bundle` output with its marketplace.json. Distinct from the standalone tag-asset step, already wired (unexercised until a tag).

acceptance: A stranger acquires temper on all three OSes without a Rust toolchain (darwin binary notarized + pinned), or via the plugin/bundle (marketplace.json); the binary self-reports 0.1.0 in lockstep with the v0.1 npm cut (distribution.md: prebuilt binaries install on all three OSes; lockstep engine pin).
notes: Release infra outside src/; cargo gates can't validate npm/bundle publish. Park re-tested on disk at 0c25b2c and holds on every clause: `git tag -l` carries only the four era tags and no version tag, crate 0.1.0 vs npm 0.0.7, and release.yml:7-9 still states the darwin + channel-3 deferral verbatim. scoped at b676656.
